content:
12 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI moves at machine speed. Mistakes do too. Generated with ChatGPT.Claude Code once suggested something that immediately made me stop:
git push --force
It was trying to help me resolve a merge conflict, and from its perspective, the suggestion made sense. For a few seconds, I almost accepted it.
Then I realized the real problem was not that Claude suggested the wrong command. The problem was that my setup allowed it to get that close. I was relying on an instruction like:
“Don’t run dangerous commands.”
But instructions are not guarantees. A model can misunderstand context. A prompt can fail. A rule written in a file can be ignored. Anything truly dangerous should not depend on the model making the right decision every single time. That moment changed how I looked at Claude Code.
I stopped treating it like a smarter chat window where I type better prompts. I started treating it like an engineering system that needs guardrails, configuration, memory, and workflows before the first prompt is ever written.
These are the things I now set up on every new project. Most take only a few minutes. But they completely change what you get back from Claude Code.

Start With /init, But Don’t Trust Everything It Creates
The fastest way to create a useful CLAUDE.md file is actually not writing it yourself.
Let Claude create the first version. Inside your project, run:
/init
Claude scans your project, understands your tech stack, detects your package manager, finds your test commands, and creates a starter CLAUDE.md file in less than a minute.
But don’t treat that file as finished. Think of it as a 70% draft. And a 70% draft that you clean up is much better than staring at an empty file you keep planning to write later.
The cleanup step is where most people go wrong. For every line Claude generates, ask one question:
“If I remove this, will Claude make worse decisions?”
If the answer is no, delete it. A bigger CLAUDE.md does not make Claude smarter. It usually just adds more noise and makes the important instructions harder to follow.
The things I always keep are the project-specific details:
## Build & Run
- Build: `npm run build`
- Test: `npm test -- --watchAll=false`
- Lint: `npm run lint -- --fix`## Conventions
- TypeScript strict mode, no `any`
- No default exports
- All API responses wrapped in `{ data, error, meta }`
## Critical Rules
- Never modify files in `/generated` - these are auto-produced
- Always run lint before marking a task done
Notice what is missing:
No “write clean code.” No “follow best practices.” No generic programming advice.
Claude already understands those things. Your CLAUDE.md should contain the information it cannot guess — your project’s commands, decisions, restrictions, and conventions. I try to keep the main file under 200 lines. So far, I have never needed more.

Keep CLAUDE.md Small by Moving Details Somewhere Else
One mistake I made early was treating CLAUDE.md like a place to store everything.
Build commands. Architecture notes. API rules. Database details.
Every piece of documentation went into one huge file. Then I wondered why Claude sometimes missed the important parts. The problem was simple: everything looked important, so nothing stood out.
Your main CLAUDE.md should only contain things Claude needs in almost every session:

• commands it will run repeatedly

• project conventions

• rules that should always apply
Anything large or specific to one part of the codebase should live somewhere else and be referenced when needed. That is where imports help.
# CLAUDE.md (root — stays lean)
## Project
- Stack: Python 3.12, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Redis
- Build: `make build` | Test: `make test` | Lint: `make lint`## Architecture
@docs/architecture.md
## API Standards
@docs/api-standards.md
## Database Schema
@docs/schema.md
Now the main file stays small. Instead of carrying your entire architecture, API documentation, and database schema into every conversation, Claude can pull in the details when they actually matter. The result is simple:
Less noise. Cleaner context. Better responses.

Don’t Just Tell Claude What Not To Do. Block It
This was the first thing I changed after Claude suggested running a force push. At first, I thought adding rules inside CLAUDE.md would be enough. Something like:
“Never run dangerous commands.”
And most of the time, that works. But “most of the time” is not good enough when the command can delete files, overwrite history, or change production data. The problem is that instructions depend on the model making the right decision every single time. Guardrails don’t.
That’s where PreToolUse hooks become useful.
A hook runs before Claude executes a tool call. It can inspect the action and block anything unsafe before it happens. For example:
# .claude/hooks/safety-gate.sh#!/bin/bash
# PreToolUse hook - blocks dangerous bash patterns before they run
TOOL_NAME="$1"
TOOL_INPUT="$2"
if [ "$TOOL_NAME" = "Bash" ]; then
# Block force pushes
if echo "$TOOL_INPUT" | grep -qE 'git push.*--force|git push.*-f'; then
echo "Force push blocked. Use --force-with-lease or open a PR." >&2
exit 2
fi
# Block recursive deletes on non-temp paths
if echo "$TOOL_INPUT" | grep -qE 'rm\s+-rf\s+(?!(/tmp|./tmp))'; then
echo "rm -rf outside /tmp is blocked. Delete files explicitly." >&2
exit 2
fi
# Block production DB operations without explicit confirmation flag
if echo "$TOOL_INPUT" | grep -qE 'DROP TABLE|TRUNCATE|DELETE FROM' ; then
echo "Destructive DB operation blocked. Run manually with confirmation." >&2
exit 2
fi
fi
exit 0
Registered in .claude/settings.json:
{
"hooks": {
"PreToolUse": [
{
"matcher": "*",
"hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "bash .claude/hooks/safety-gate.sh" }]
}
]
}
}After that, safety no longer depends on Claude remembering an instruction. The command is stopped before execution. That’s the mindset shift:
Prompts guide behavior. Systems enforce boundaries. For anything that can cause real damage, enforcement always beats asking nicely.

Stop Repeating Prompts. Turn Them Into Commands
One pattern I noticed quickly. I was typing the same instructions again and again.
Review this PR.
Check for security issues.
Look for missing error handling.
Ignore formatting because the linter handles that.
After repeating the same prompt a few times, I realized something:
If I have to explain a workflow repeatedly, it probably shouldn’t be a prompt anymore. It should become a command.
Claude Code lets you create custom slash commands as simple markdown files inside .claude/commands/.
For example, this is one I use for PR reviews:
<!-- .claude/commands/review-pr.md -->---
argument-hint: [PR number]
description: Review a pull request for logic errors, security issues, and missing error handling.
---
Fetch the PR diff for #$ARGUMENTS using `gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS`.
Review it for:
- Logic errors and edge cases
- Security vulnerabilities (injection, auth bypass, data exposure)
- Missing error handling or unhappy paths
- Missing or insufficient tests
Do NOT comment on style, naming conventions, or formatting - those are handled by the linter.
Produce output as three sections: **Critical** (must fix before merge),
**Important** (should fix), **Minor** (optional).
Every PR after that is one line:
> /review-pr 342No rewriting the same instructions. No forgetting important checks. No slightly different prompts producing different review styles.
Thirty seconds of setup turns a repeated prompt into a reusable workflow.

Make Claude Plan Before It Starts Changing Code
One of the biggest mistakes I made early was letting Claude jump straight into implementation. For small changes, that usually worked.
For anything touching multiple files, it became risky. Claude might edit the right files in the wrong order, misunderstand the architecture, or solve a slightly different problem than the one I actually wanted.
That’s why I use plan mode for larger tasks.
> /plan Refactor the auth module to support OAuth2 alongside our existing JWT flowBefore changing anything, Claude explains:

• which files it wants to modify

• why those files need changes

• the order it plans to follow

• what the final result should look like
Only after reviewing that plan do I let it continue. It feels like an extra step, but it saves time. Reviewing a plan for two minutes is much easier than reviewing a bad implementation spread across fifteen files.
I also built one simple habit: If Claude starts editing before explaining the approach, I stop it.
> Stop. Before you make any changes, tell me your plan: which files you'll touch,
in what order, and what you expect each change to do.That small pause has prevented more wasted context, unnecessary rewrites, and wrong implementations than almost anything else.
Fast execution is useful. But only after you know it’s moving in the right direction.

Manage Context Before Claude Starts Forgetting Important Things
One mistake I made early was assuming Claude could keep everything in memory forever. It can’t.
Context is a limited workspace. If it gets too full, Claude starts losing track of important details. But clearing it too often means throwing away useful information the model still needs.
The trick is knowing when to compress and when to restart. I usually check where things stand with:
> /contextWhen the context gets too large, I use /compact. But the important part is not just compacting.
It’s telling Claude what information must survive. For example:
# Not ideal — Claude decides what matters
> /compact# Better — you control what gets preserved
> /compact Preserve:
- API changes and why they were made
- Modified files
- Errors we found and how we fixed them
Compress:
- Failed attempts
- Old exploration paths
Without instructions, you’re trusting Claude to decide what future Claude will need. Sometimes it gets that right. Sometimes it removes exactly the detail you needed later.
That’s why I also keep compact rules directly inside CLAUDE.md.
## Compact InstructionsWhen summarizing this conversation, always preserve:
- Schema changes and migration decisions
- Errors encountered and their solutions
- Files changed during the current task
Compress:
- Exploration
- Abandoned approaches
The goal is not just reducing context size. The goal is keeping the information that still matters.

Let Subagents Explore Before Your Main Agent Starts Working
One thing I learned quickly:
Exploring a codebase is expensive. Before Claude writes a single line of code, it often needs to understand the project first. It reads files. Searches for patterns. Checks history. Looks through old implementations.
All of that information is useful, but it can quickly fill your main context with temporary details you may never need again. That’s where subagents help.
A subagent gets its own separate context window. It can do the messy exploration work and return only the important findings back to your main session.
For every unfamiliar codebase, I use an explorer agent:
# .claude/agents/explorer.md---
name: explorer
description: Read-only codebase exploration. Use to map architecture, trace data flows,
or understand how a module works - without polluting the main context.
tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Bash(find *), Bash(git log *), Bash(git grep *)
model: sonnet
---
You are a codebase cartographer. Your job is to read and understand, never to edit.
When exploring:
1. Start with package.json / pyproject.toml / go.mod to understand dependencies
2. Map the top-level directory structure
3. Trace the entry points
4. Identify the main data models
5. Follow the path of one representative request end-to-end
Return a structured summary: architecture overview, key modules and their
responsibilities, data flow, and the 3 things a new engineer most needs to know.
Then I can ask:
> @explorer Map how user authentication flows from the login endpoint
through to session creationThe subagent might read dozens of files
